content:
An attack with the ranged keyword ignores the retaliate keyword.

  • The ranged keyword is equivalent to the following ability: “This attack ignores the retaliate keyword.”

Cards enter play in a ready state, positioned so that their controller can read their text from left to right.

  • If a player is instructed to ready an exhausted card, the card is returned to its ready state.
    • If there is an additional cost for a player to ready a card, that player can choose not to pay that cost. If they do not pay the cost, the card does not ready.
See: Basic Power, Recover, Recovery.

Recovery is a basic power a player can use in alter-ego form. To recover, the player exhausts their alter-ego and heals a number of hit points equal to their REC value.

  • An identity that has no damage to heal cannot perform a basic recovery.

Some abilities refer to specific cards by name (referential abilities). If a title is shared by multiple cards, the ability refers only to the highest-priority match:

  1. The card on which it's printed (self-referential).
  2. Cards associated with the same identity (identity card, identity-specific cards, obligation cards, nemesis set, side deck cards).
  3. Player cards (if on a player card) or encounter cards (if on an encounter card).

A character's remaining hit points is the amount of damage that character can take before reaching zero hit points. An identity's/villain's hit point dial represents remaining hit points.

For an ally/minion, subtract sustained damage from maximum hit points.
Some card abilities may include reminder text, italicized and in parentheses. Reminder text has no effect on the game and only reminds a player of a specific game function or rule.

A card removed from the game is set aside and does not interact with the game for the duration of its removal (until the end of the game if unspecified). 'Removed from the game' is an out-of-play state; a card that has been removed cannot reenter the game through any means.

A replacement effect replaces a specified effect with a different effect. Most are interrupt abilities in the format 'when [triggering condition] would happen, do [replacement effect] instead.'

When an effect is replaced, it is no longer considered imminent and no further interrupts/responses to it can be triggered.

A card with the requirement keyword cannot be played unless each resource of the specified type is spent while paying for its cost. Equivalent to: 'When paying this card's resource cost, you must spend the following resources: [resources].'

A card with the requirement keyword cannot be played 'ignoring its resource cost.'

Effects, abilities, event cards, and treachery cards are each resolved under specific conditions: an effect resolves when applied to the game state; an ability resolves when triggered and effects resolve; an event resolves when played and abilities resolve; a treachery resolves when revealed and abilities resolve. Constant abilities are never considered resolved (always active while in play).

  • Card types other than events/treacheries are not considered resolved, though abilities on them can be.
  • If all effects of an ability are canceled, it is not considered to have resolved.

• Resources are used to pay the cost to play cards and pay certain ability costs.
  • A player generates resources by discarding cards from hand (generating the resource indicated at bottom-left) or using card abilities that generate resources. Four types: energy, mental, physical, and wild (wild can be used as its type or any other). To pay a cost, resources generated must equal or exceed the cost; abilities may require specific types in specific quantities.
  • Excess resources generated toward any cost are lost and do not carry over.

A resource ability is a triggered ability indicated by the bold 'Resource' timing trigger, triggerable anytime the controlling player is generating resources to pay a cost. A resource ability generating a resource only for a specific type of cost cannot be triggered when paying a cost not of that type.

Resource cards are a player card type whose primary function is being discarded from hand to generate resources, generally providing more (or more efficient) resources than other card types. Some have card text active while using them to generate resources.

Resource cards are considered an extension of an identity — if a player spends a resource, it's also considered spent by their identity.
See: Resource.

• A response ability is a triggered ability indicated by the bold 'Response' timing trigger, resolved after the specified triggering condition occurs.
  • Players can only trigger response abilities on cards they control or encounter cards.
  • Multiple responses may trigger from the same condition but each only once per occurrence; multiple copies can each trigger.
  • If a single effect causes multiple triggering conditions, responses to each can be resolved in any order.
  • Once all players decline further responses, further responses to that instance cannot be used.

A player cannot have more than two cards with the restricted keyword in play under their control at the same time. A player can play/put into play a restricted card even at two already, but must then immediately discard from play restricted cards until only two remain.

  • Equivalent to:
    Forced Response: After you take control of this card, if you control more than 2 restricted cards, discard all but 2 of those cards.

After a character with the retaliate X keyword is attacked, deal X damage to the attacker.

  • Equivalent to:
    Forced Response: After this character is attacked, deal X damage to the attacker.
  • The character with retaliate X must be in play after the attack resolves to deal this damage.

During step four of the villain phase, each player (in player order) reveals and resolves all facedown encounter cards dealt to them, one at a time in the order dealt. To reveal:

  1. Turn the card faceup.
  2. Handle per card type: Attachment enters play attached per its 'attach to' text (or on the table, not in play, if none); Environment enters the villain's play area; Minion enters the revealing player's area, engaging that player; Obligation enters the revealing player's area (or the specified player's); Side scheme enters the villain's play area; Treachery/Other placed on the table (not in play).
  3. Resolve each 'When Revealed' ability on the card.
  4. If a treachery, discard it. Responses to any step of revealing are not resolved until after all steps of the reveal process are completed.
See: Modes of Play.
See: Round Overview, End of Player Phase, Player Phase, Player Turn, Villain Phase.
See: Encounter Deck, Player Deck.
